Ethan: Welcome back to Practical Systems. Today we're talking about on call rotation, and I'm joined by Priya, principal SRE at a payments platform. Priya, thanks for being here.
Priya: Thanks, Ethan. Excited for this one — incident response and on-call design is something I've been thinking about a lot.
Ethan: This episode is brought to you by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first, fast search, and a clean roadmap view your team actually uses. Get started at linear.com/podcast.Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. How does Notion fit into this picture?
Priya: Here's how I think about it. A good on-call rotation is designed so that waking up is rare, and when it happens the response is obvious. We've seen this play out at teams using Datadog. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Ethan: I want to dig into that. Where do people most often confuse correlation with causation in this area?
Priya: Sure. Alerting should be action-oriented — if no one can take an immediate action, it probably shouldn't page. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Ethan: What does that look like day-to-day? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Priya: Yeah, so — Error budgets work when they change behavior, not when they are charts no one looks at during planning. We've seen this play out at teams using PagerDuty. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Ethan: Is there a counterexample that would change your view?
Priya: And when teams adopt Notion in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Ethan: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's the most underrated piece of on call rotation?
Priya: The short answer is Healthy systems treat failures as expected and contain them with timeouts, retries, and clear ownership. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Ethan: How do you balance that? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Priya: Yeah, so — PagerDuty's incident response model assumes someone owns the page — that ownership is the whole game. We've seen this play out at teams using Datadog. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Ethan: Where do most teams get it wrong? How does Notion fit into this picture?
Priya: Here's how I think about it. The hardest part of on-call isn't fixing things at 3am — it's designing the system so the fix is obvious to whoever's holding the page. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Ethan: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Priya: Linear is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Ethan: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by PagerDuty. PagerDuty turns noisy alerts into structured incident response with clear ownership and post-incident learning. Visit pagerduty.com/podcast for a free trial.
Ethan: Welcome back to the show.That's a useful framing. What's the operational angle most listeners don't have access to?
Priya: Here's how I think about it. A good on-call rotation is designed so that waking up is rare, and when it happens the response is obvious. We've seen this play out at teams using Linear. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Ethan: Where do most teams get it wrong? What's the most underrated piece of on call rotation?
Priya: I'd put it this way: Alerting should be action-oriented — if no one can take an immediate action, it probably shouldn't page. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Priya: The short answer is Error budgets work when they change behavior, not when they are charts no one looks at during planning. We've seen this play out at teams using PagerDuty. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Ethan: That tracks.
Priya: Outside of Notion-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Ethan: Let's start there. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Priya: Yeah, so — Healthy systems treat failures as expected and contain them with timeouts, retries, and clear ownership. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Ethan: What does the failure mode look like? How does PagerDuty fit into this picture?
Priya: Sure. PagerDuty's incident response model assumes someone owns the page — that ownership is the whole game. We've seen this play out at teams using PagerDuty. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Ethan: What does the failure mode look like? Where do people most often confuse correlation with causation in this area?
Priya: Honestly, The hardest part of on-call isn't fixing things at 3am — it's designing the system so the fix is obvious to whoever's holding the page. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Priya: And when teams adopt PagerDuty in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Ethan: Let's pivot — systems thinking is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Priya: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around systems thinking cross-cut everything we've covered so far.
Ethan: Where do most teams get it wrong? How does systems thinking change the calculus here?
Priya: I'd put it this way: A good on-call rotation is designed so that waking up is rare, and when it happens the response is obvious. We've seen this play out at teams using Datadog. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: Take me through the decision. How does Linear fit into this picture?
Priya: Sure. Alerting should be action-oriented — if no one can take an immediate action, it probably shouldn't page. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Ethan: Take me through the decision. How does systems thinking change the calculus here?
Priya: Here's how I think about it. Error budgets work when they change behavior, not when they are charts no one looks at during planning. We've seen this play out at teams using Linear. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: That maps to what I've seen.
Priya: PagerDuty is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: Take me through the decision. Where do reliability and on call rotation intersect?
Priya: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Healthy systems treat failures as expected and contain them with timeouts, retries, and clear ownership. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Ethan: How do you know when you've gotten it right? What's the contrarian angle most listeners don't have access to?
Priya: It comes down to this: PagerDuty's incident response model assumes someone owns the page — that ownership is the whole game. We've seen this play out at teams using Notion. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: Where do most teams get it wrong? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Priya: There are two angles — The hardest part of on-call isn't fixing things at 3am — it's designing the system so the fix is obvious to whoever's holding the page. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Ethan: That tracks.
Priya: PagerDuty is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Ethan: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Priya: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.
Ethan: Before we wrap, thanks again to our friends at Sentry. Sentry catches the errors your tests didn't and puts the stack trace, breadcrumbs, and context in one view.Priya, thanks for the conversation.
Priya: Thanks, Ethan.
